coredata.dc:title: An ultra-sensitive analytical platform based on bluish green emitting carbon quantum dots for the detection of curcumin in dietary foods
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Lingfei
Hu, Qin
Sun, Huijuan
Han, Jie
Pan, Yangni
Yang, Zhen-quan
coredata.dc:description: This work reports the development of a new analytical method for the rapid detection of curcumin in food samples by using bluish green emissive fluorescence carbon quantum dots (BG-CQDs) as nanoprobe. The sensing mechanism behind the detection work is the quenching effect of curcumin on the fluorescence intensity of BG-CQDs, where curcumin and BG-CQDs interact via a combined effect of inner filter effect (IFE) and dynamic interaction process. After optimisation of the experimental parameters, the developed analytical method is capable of detecting curcumin with good selectivity and high sensitivity, achieving good linearity in the concentration range of 0.01–25.0 μM. A limit of detection (LOD) of 28.7 nM was obtained, which is lower than that of any of the previously reported CQDs-based fluorescence methods. The developed analytical method was applied to the analyses of food samples with a high degree of accuracy and precision. This work highlights a robust BG-CQDs-based analytical platform of rapid response, low cost, great simplicity, high selectivity and favourable sensitivity, which exhibits immense potential for the accurate monitoring of the curcumin content in dietary food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Method development
Fluorescence sensing
Curcumin
Carbon quantum dots
Food analysis

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical performance test of the proposed analytical method
Sample preparation and curcumin content determination
Characterisation of the physical and chemical properties of the as-prepared BG-CQDs
Investigation of the linear range and limit of detection of the proposed analytical method
Interference studies of the proposed sensing platform
Application of the proposed analytical method for real samples analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthesis of B,G-CQDs and the interaction between B,G-CQDs and curcumin.
Morphology and size measurement. (A) The TEM image and (B) the histogram for particle size distribution calculated by randomly counting about 80 particles on the TEM image of B,G-CQDs.

Spectral properties study. (A) UV–vis absorption spectrum (blue line), excitation (black curve) and emission spectra (red curve) of B,G-CQDs. The inset on the top-right is the picture of a B,G-CQDs solution under UV illumination. (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of B,G-CQDs at various λex ranging from 300 nm to 500 nm.

Optimisation of sensing conditions. (A) The plot of F0/F as a function of B,G-CQDs concentration. The B,G-CQDs solutions were prepared by dissolving appropriate amounts of B,G-CQDs in PBS solution (pH 9.0, 10.0 mM) in a concentration range of 0.01‒1.0 mg/mL. (B) The changes of F0/F as a function of pH. The B,G-CQDs (0.05 mg/mL) were prepared in PBS solutions (10.0 mM) at different pH values. (C) The plot of F0/F versus response time. The B,G-CQDs solution was prepared in PBS solution (pH 9.0, 10.0 mM) at a concentration of 0.05 mg/mL. For all the experiments, the content of curcumin used is 20.0 μM.

Fluorescence quenching observation and linearity test. (A) The emission spectra of a B,G-CQDs solution (2.0 mL, 0.05 mg/mL) containing various concentrations of curcumin (1‒24: 0, 0.1, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 10.0, 12.0, 15.0, 20.0, 25.0, 27.0, 30.0, 32.0, 35.0, 37.0, 40.0, 42.0, 45.0, 47.0 and 50.0 μM). (B) The SV plot of F0/F against the concentration of curcumin. All assays were performed in quintuples and the error bars show the corresponding standard deviation (n = 5).

Selectivity and anti-interference tests. (A) The F0/F of a B,G-CQDs solution (2.0 mL, 0.05 mg/mL) containing various chemical species at a concentration level of 0.1 mM, respectively. (B) The F0/F of a B,G-CQDs (0.05 mg/mL)/curcumin (0.1 mM) sensing system measured before and after mixing with various competing substances (0.1 mM), respectively. All assays were performed in quintuples and the error bars show the corresponding standard deviation (n = 5).

Fluorescence quenching mechanism investigation. (A) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of curcumin (black curve) and the excitation (blue curve) and emission (red curve) spectra of B,G-CQDs. (B) The fluorescence decay curves of a B,G-CQDs solution (0.05 mg/mL) in the absence (blue line) and in the presence (red line) of 20.0 μM curcumin. The measurements were carried out at a λex/λem of 400/490 nm.
Comparison of different analytical methods for detection of curcumin.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: P,N-CQDs: phosphorous and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots.
B,N-CQDs: boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots.
P,N,B-CQDs: phosphorous, boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots.
N,S-CQDs: nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The analysis results for curcumin determination in food samples by using the proposed analytical method.

head.ce:title: An ultra-sensitive analytical platform based on bluish green emitting carbon quantum dots for the detection of curcumin in dietary foods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Postdoctoral research fellow on visit to School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports the development of a new analytical method for the rapid detection of curcumin in food samples by using bluish green emissive fluorescence carbon quantum dots (BG-CQDs) as nanoprobe. The sensing mechanism behind the detection work is the quenching effect of curcumin on the fluorescence intensity of BG-CQDs, where curcumin and BG-CQDs interact via a combined effect of inner filter effect (IFE) and dynamic interaction process. After optimisation of the experimental parameters, the developed analytical method is capable of detecting curcumin with good selectivity and high sensitivity, achieving good linearity in the concentration range of 0.01–25.0 μM. A limit of detection (LOD) of 28.7 nM was obtained, which is lower than that of any of the previously reported CQDs-based fluorescence methods. The developed analytical method was applied to the analyses of food samples with a high degree of accuracy and precision. This work highlights a robust BG-CQDs-based analytical platform of rapid response, low cost, great simplicity, high selectivity and favourable sensitivity, which exhibits immense potential for the accurate monitoring of the curcumin content in dietary food samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin is a non-polar polyphenol extracted from the plant root of the Curcuma longa L (Zokhtareh and Rahimnejad, 2018), which has been used as a traditional Chinese medicine and food additive for many years. The previous research on the characteristics of curcumin has shown that curcumin is beneficial for human health and wellbeing due to its pharmacological activities, such as antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, anticarcinogenic, hepatoprotective and antidyspeptic properties (Kharat et al., 2020). As a result, curcumin has been widely applied in pharmacological use for the treatment of diabetes, allergy, depression, arthritis, acquired immune deficiency syndrome and diverse types of cancers (Afkhami et al., 2017). Nevertheless, with ever-increasing studies carried out on curcumin, the treatment of curcumin at high dose is likely to show pro-oxidant activity on DNA (Ziyatdinova et al., 2012), induce a significant increase in the frequency of chromosomal aberrations in Chinese hamster ovary cells, trigger the increase of necrosis process, and lead to the decrease of intracellular ATP levels (Chan et al., 2006). Therefore, close attention should be paid to the dietary intake amount of curcumin, and the development of a high-efficiency detection method for curcumin is of high necessity.

To date, a number of analytical techniques have been developed to detect curcumin in food samples. These techniques include spectrophotometry (Afkhami et al., 2017; Tang et al., 2002), voltammetry (Chaisiwamongkhol et al., 2017; Daneshgar et al., 2009; Wray et al., 2012; Yang et al., 2018; Zokhtareh and Rahimnejad, 2018), resonance light scattering (RLS) (Chen et al., 2012), capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Yuan et al., 2005) and liquid chromatography-based methods (LC-based) (Ansari et al., 2005; Lee and Choung, 2011; Mahale et al., 2018; Rahimi et al., 2014; Tønnesen and Karlsen, 1983). Even though those analytical methods could provide desirable accuracy, good selectivity and favorable sensitivity; unfortunately, most of them not only call for trained analysts but also require sophisticated equipment, complex sample preparation procedures and long analysis time. An alternative simple and rapid detection technique is the development of chemical sensors with a possible methodology to detect an optical signal based on an analytical sensing platform (He et al., 2018a; He et al. 2019a; He et al. 2018b; He et al. 2019b; He et al., 2019c; Wu et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2019a; Wu et al., 2019b). Recently, many attempts have been devoted to produce efficient sensors, especially fluorescence sensors (Baig and Chen, 2017; Liu et al., 2018; Shi et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2015), to accomplish a more selective, sensitive and accurate analytical approach.

Functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are recently discovered fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with typical sizes of <10 nm (Baker and Baker, 2010). As a result of their superior photophysical and chemical properties, such as tunable fluorescence emission, good photostability, broad excitation wavelengths (λex), λex-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior, low photo-bleaching, easy functionalisation and low cytotoxicity (Liu et al., 2007; Su et al., 2020), CQDs have gained considerable attention in various fluorescence sensing applications (Hu et al., 2020a; Hu et al., 2020b; Zhu et al., 2013). So far, intensive research efforts have been made and significant achievements have been reported in fabricating highly efficient CQDs-based nanoprobes for curcumin detection with good analytical performance (Baig and Chen, 2017; Bian et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2018; Shi et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2015). The sensing mechanism is based on the fluorescence suppression of CQDs, where their fluorescence can be quenched in the presence of curcumin by either Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) (Shi et al., 2015) or inner filter effect (IFE) (Bian et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2015). The former involves an intermolecular interaction between a target molecule and a nanoprobe when they have a complementary geometry or they are at a particular distance, while the later occurs when there is overlapping between the absorption band of an absorber and the excitation and/or emission band of a fluorophore (Gong et al., 2017). Given that most CQDs present a fluorescence spectrum in the short-wavelength area with blue fluorescence, they are suffering from lacking a large stokes shift or being easily disturbed by the scattering spectra of the solvent, which largely limit the sensitivity of CQDs nanosensors. A possible pathway to overcome these shortcomings is to fabricate CQDs with long-wavelength emission that is less scattered; however, research on using long-wavelength emissive CQDs as nanoprobe for curcumin detection has not been reported yet.

In this work, bluish green emissive CNPs (BG-CQDs) were for the first time fabricated by a simple one-step hydrothermal approach using m-phenylenediamine and citric acid as precursors (Fig. 1). The sensing principle for curcumin detection was based on the quenching effect of curcumin on BG-CQDs fluorescence intensity via a combined effect of IFE and dynamic interaction. The optimisation of the working conditions (i.e., the BG-CQDs dosage, pH and reaction time) and the investigation of the mechanism behind fluorescence quenching were carried out in detail. The analytical parameters of the sensing system, including linearity, limits of detection (LOD), selectivity, anti-interference ability, recovery, trueness assessment and standard uncertainty, were fully validated. Finally, the newly developed analytical method was utilized to determine curcumin in commercial food samples with high accuracy and precision. As far as we aware, this is the first time that a BG-CQDs-based fluorescence sensing platform was fabricated for the ultra-sensitive detection of curcumin in complex food samples. It is anticipated that the newly developed analytical method would provide a basis for the building of portable nanodevices for curcumin monitoring in complicated dietary foods.

Citric acid, m-phenylenediamine, curcumin and glutathione (GSH) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Ascorbic acid and glucose were obtained from Chongqing Maoye Chemicals (Chongqing, China). Na2HPO4·12H2O, NaH2PO4·2H2O, NaOH, NaCl and NaHCO3 were bought from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All amino acids and other reagents and chemicals were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was produced by a Milli-Q ultrapure water system (Millipore, Billerica, USA) with the water outlet operating at 18.2 M.

A one-step hydrothermal method with m-phenylenediamine and citric acid as precursors was used for BG-CQDs synthesis. In detail, 0.05 g of m-phenylenediamine was firstly added into a mixture solution of 5.0 mL of double distilled water and 5.0 mL of anhydrous ethanol, and 0.088 g of anhydrous citric acid was added to mix. Afterwards, the mixture was subjected to sonication for 2.0 min, transferred into a 30.0 mL Teflon container, sealed in a stainless steel autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 12 h in an electric oven. After being cooled down to room temperature, the resulting mixture was diluted with 10.0 mL of ultrapure water and dialyzed against 1.0 L of ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 500–1000 Da, Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China)) for 3 days. During this step, magnetic stirring was constantly applied and fresh ultrapure water was recharged every 24 h. After being purified by dialysis, the clear brown BG-CQDs solution inside the membrane tube was collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance test of the proposed analytical method
ce:section.ce:para: The linear range of the analytical method was assessed by continuously adding various amounts of curcumin into a BG-CQDs solution (2.0 mL, 0.05 mg/mL), and the added contents of curcumin in the BG-CQDs solution ranged from 0.01 to 25.0 μM. Afterwards, the fluorescence intensities of the BG-CQDs solution containing various concentrations of curcumin were measured at a λex/emission wavelength (λem) of 400/490 nm. The calibration curves were established by plotting the quenching efficiency of fluorescence (F0/F; F0 and F refer to the fluorescence intensity of the BG-CQDs solution without and with the presence of curcumin, respectively) versus the concentration of curcumin in the BG-CQDs solution. All assays were performed in quintuples, i.e., n = 5.

The selectivity of the analytical method was studied by recording the fluorescence intensities of a set of BG-CQDs solutions (2.0 mL, 0.05 mg/mL) before and after mixing with 13 kinds of anions (F-, Cl-, Br-, I-, IO3-, Ac-, NO3-, NO2-, HCO3-, CO3 2-, SO4 2-, HPO4 2- and H2PO4-), 8 kinds of cations (Na+, K+, NH4 +, Ag+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe2+ and Mn2+), 8 kinds of amino acids (alanine, DL-cysteine, glycine, glutamate, threonine, methionine, histidine and serine) and 4 kinds of small molecules (curcumin, GSH, glucose and ascorbic acid), respectively. The concentration of each added substances was kept at 0.1 mM. The λex and λem were set at 400 and 490 nm, respectively.

The anti-interference ability of the analytical method was then investigated. A BG-CQDs solution (2.0 mL, 0.05 mg/mL) containing 0.1 mM curcumin was firstly prepared. After that, each of the above mentioned competing substances was introduced into the BG-CQDs/curcumin system, respectively, and the concentration of each competing species was kept at 0.1 mM. The fluorescence measurements of the blank BG-CQDs solution and the BG-CQDs/curcumin mixture solutions containing different potential competing susbstances were made with the λex and λem being set at 400 and 490 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation and curcumin content determination
ce:section.ce:para: Four samples (red paprika powder, ginger powder, mustard powder and turmeric powder) were purchased from local markets. Firstly, 0.1 g of each sample was extracted by 1.0 mL of ethanol with the assistance of 1 min of vigorous vortexing. After that, each mixture was centrifuged at 15,000 rpm in a TG16A-WS high speed centrifuge (Luxiangyi Centrifuge Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) for 15 min. The supernatants of each treated sample were then collected and diluted with ethanol for 5, 3, 1 and 30 times, respectively. Afterwards, a volume of 10.0 μL of each sample extraction was added into a BG-CQDs solution (2.0 mL, 0.05 mg/mL), respectively. Finally, the fluorescence measurements of the BG-CQDs solution before and after mixing with the sample extraction were made with the λex and λem being set at 400 and 490 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterisation of the physical and chemical properties of the as-prepared BG-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size of BG-CQDs were characterised by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 2 A shows the TEM image of BG-CQDs. It is observed that the BG-CQDs are dispersed rather evenly on the TEM grid and exhibit a spherical structure. The particle size distribution histogram of BG-CQDs (Fig. 2B) showed that the BG-CQDs species were evenly distributed with an average particle size of approximately 4.5 nm. In addition, dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement was performed to assess the hydrodynamic particle size of BG-CQDs. As shown in Fig. S1, BG-CQDs had an average hydrodynamic particle size of 6.72 ± 0.6 nm, indicating the high colloidal stability of BG-CQDs in the aqueous phase. The BG-CQDs were found to be homogenous in aqueous phase without any noticeable aggregation within two months at ambient conditions.

The surface functionality of BG-CQDs was investigated by FTIR (Fig. S2). The absorption peaks at 3359 and 3222 cm−1are ascribed to O–H and N–H stretching, respectively. The stretching of C–H and C = O at 2980 and 1708 cm−1indicate the presence of alkyl and carboxylic groups, respectively. The bending of N–H and the stretching of C–N were observed at the sharp absorption peaks of 1601 and 1487 cm−1, respectively. The peak at 1395 cm−1 is attributed to C–O stretching, which implies the partial oxidisation of the BG-CQDs surface. The detail elemental components of BG-CQDs were determined by elemental analysis (Table S1). The as-prepared BG-CQDs were composed of 61.88% carbon (C), 18.2% oxygen (O), 6.17% hydrogen (H) and 13.75% N. In essence, the surface of BG-CQDs is functionalized with amine, amide, hydroxyl and carboxylic groups, which affords the good water solubility of BG-CQDs.

The charge states of BG-CQDs in aqueous phase at various pH values (2.0–11.0) were investigated by zeta potential measurement (Fig. S3). The BG-CQDs were positively charged at pH 2.0–3.0 and negatively charged at pH 4.0–12.0. These observations are due to that the protonation of the surface-attached amine groups on BG-CQDs plays a dominant role at low pH (2.0–3.0) while the dissociation of carboxylic acid groups will be promoted at high pH (4.0–12.0), which imparts the hydrophilicity and colloidal stability of BG-CQDs.

The UV–vis and PL properties of BG-CQDs were then investigated. The UV-vis spectrum of BG-CQDs (Fig. 3 A, blue curve) showed that the BG-CQDs had typical absorption at 293 and 376 nm, respectively, which are ascribed to the n→π* transition of the C = O bonding and the surface emissive traps from the doping of N heteroatoms, respectively (Gong et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2020a). The excitation spectrum of BG-CQDs (Fig. 3A, black curve) revealed an excitation band at 400 nm. The fluorescence emission spectrum of BG-CQDs (Fig. 3A, red curve) indicated an emission peak at 490 nm upon excitation at 400 nm, exhibiting bright bluish green emission under UV irradiation (inset of Fig. 3A). Specifically, the PL of BG-CQDs (Fig. 3B) showed λex-dependent PL behaviour, which is ascribed to the “surface states” due to the size effect and the presence of various functionalities on the BG-CQDs surface (Shang et al., 2012; Tang et al., 2012). The emission bands of BG-CQDs vary in the range of 473‒528 nm (λex = 300‒500 nm), and the strongest emission was obtained at a λex of 400 nm. Using quinine sulphate as a reference, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of BG-CQDs was determined to be 61.7% (Fig. S4).

The BG-CQDs concentration, pH and reaction time were reported to play key roles in governing the quenching efficiency (F0/F) of a fluorescence sensing system (Liu et al., 2015; Na et al., 2019). As such, the effects of BG-CQDs, pH and reaction time on F0/F were extensively investigated. An addition of 20.0 μM curcumin into the BG-CQD solution was used for the experiment. This concentration was within the linear range (vide infra) and can cause an obvious decrease in the fluorescence of BG-CQD solution.

Firstly, the BG-CQDs solutions with the concentrations ranging from 0.01 to 1.0 mg/mL were used to inspect the influence of the BG-CQDs concentration on F0/F (Fig. 4 A). Since the fluorescence intensity of BG-CQDs (F0) is concentration-dependent (Fig. S5), the fluctuation of F0/F with the changes in the BG-CQDs concentration was observed. Increasing the concentration of BG-CQDs from 0.01 to 0.05 mg/mL results in around 1.3-fold increase in F0/F. However, further increase of the BG-CQDs dosage (0.05‒1.0 mg/mL) caused the gradual decrease in F0/F. Hence, the subsequent work was carried out with the optimal concentration of BG-CQDs at 0.05 mg/mL.

Then, the response of the sensing system towards pH was studied by observing the changes in F0/F with pH (Fig. 4B). It was observed that the sensing system is very sensitive to pH; the F0/F fluctuated when pH changed from 3.0 to 11.0, with the maximum value achieved at pH 9.0. As such, pH 9.0 was chosen as the optimal pH value in the subsequent experiments.

Finally, the response time of BG-CQDs towards curcumin was studied by monitoring the changes in F0/F with the reaction time (Fig. 4C). The F0/F gradually increased within 4.0 min after the addition of 20.0 μM curcumin into the BG-CQDs solution and remained almost stable thereafter, which indicates that the interaction between BG-CQDs and curcumin could be completed within 4.0 min. Therefore, the subsequent experiments used 1.0 min as the optimal response time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the linear range and limit of detection of the proposed analytical method
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensities of a BG-CQDs solution (0.05 mg/mL) containing different contents of curcumin were recorded to measure the linearity of the proposed analytical method. As depicted in Fig. 5 A, when increased contents of curcumin were introduced into the BG-CQDs solution, the fluorescence intensity of the BG-CQDs solution gradually decreased, indicating that curcumin could lead to a dramatical quenching effect on the fluorescence of BG-CQDs. To determine the linearity of the proposed analytical method for curcumin sensing, F0/F was plotted versus the curcumin content. A good linearity was observed in the concentration range of 0.1–25.0 μM (Fig. 5B). As shown in Fig. 5B, the corresponding calibration equation was calculated to be F0/F = 0.037[Conc.]+1.018 with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9995.

The LOD was calculated to be 28.7 μM according to the IUPAC recommendation (three times the standard deviation of 12 repeated measurements of the blank. The analytical performance of the proposed sensing platform was compared with that of other CQDs-based fluorescence methods. As shown in Table 1 , the proposed BG-CQDs-based fluorescence method achieved much lower LOD than any of the previously reported CQDs-based fluorescence sensors with a comparable linear range, indicating that the proposed fluorescence method in this work is of high sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference studies of the proposed sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate selectivity, the fluorescence responses of a BG-CQDs solution towards the introduction of various possible interfering species, including different kinds of anions, cations, amino acids and small molecules, were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 6 A, only curcumin showed dramatic quenching effect on the fluorescence of BG-CQDs while the other possible interfering species, which are common substances existing in food samples, showed inconspicuous influence on the fluorescence intensity of BG-CQDs.

In the anti-interference ability assessment, the influences of various possible competing species, i.e., different kinds of anions, cations, amino acids and small molecules, on the fluorescence intensity of the BG-CQDs/curcumin sensing system were recorded. As shown in Fig. 6B, no obvious changes were caused by the introduction of competing substances, revealing that the proposed sensing system exhibited excellent anti-interference ability towards other possible competing substances.

To understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the attempts were firstly given to study if the interaction between BG-CQDs and curcumin is a result of FRET or IFE. The excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of BG-CQDs as well as the absorption spectrum of curcumin were recorded and are displayed in Fig. 7 A. The curcumin (black curve) exhibited wide absorption covering from 240 to 600 nm. The blue curve in Fig. 7A illustrates the excitation spectrum of BG-CQDs; a maximum excitation peak at 400 nm was observed for BG-CQDs. The emission spectrum of BG-CQDs exhibited an emission band (red curve, Fig. 7A) at 490 nm at a λex of 400 nm. It is shown that the absorption spectrum of curcumin preciously overlaps with the excitation spectrum of the BG-CQDs and shows partial overlap with the emission spectrum of the BG-CQDs; therefore, the quenching effect of curcumin on BG-CQDs fluorescence is resulted from IFE.

From the other side, a fluorescence quenching process can generally be classified as static quenching and dynamic quenching (Bian et al., 2018). The fluorescence decay spectra (Fig. 7B) of a BG-CQDs solution in the absence and in the presence of 20.0 μM curcumin were recorded. The corresponding lifetimes were estimated by bi-exponential fitting (see supplementary material), and the corresponding data are shown in Table S2. The average lifetime of BG-CQDs before mixing with curcumin was 7.58 ns; however, after the addition of 20.0 μM curcumin, the average lifetime of BG-CQDs decreased to 3.98 ns. The lifetime decrease of BG-CQDs caused by the addition of curcumin implies that the fluorescence quenching of BG-CQDs by curcumin is a dynamic interaction process. In essence, the suppression of curcumin on the fluorescence of CQDs is due to a combined effect of IFE and dynamic interaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the proposed analytical method for real samples analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The developed analytical method was applied for the determination of curcumin content in various food samples. Given the high sensitivity and excellent selectivity of this fluorescence sensing system, complex food matrixes could be investigated directly with minimal sample pre-treatment. The recovery test was performed by spiking known amounts of curcumin into the food sample extractions. As shown in Table 2 , after dilution, the contents of curcumin in the extractions of food samples, i.e., red paprika powder, ginger powder, mustard powder and turmeric powder, were 1.54, 1.52, 2.27 and 2.29 μM, respectively; the corresponding concentrations in the extractions of the three samples before dilution were 7.7, 4.56, 2.27 and 68.7 μM, respectively. Table 2 shows that the recoveries were achieved in a range of 98.3‒105.7% and the relative standard deviations (RSDs), i.e., the standard uncertainty (Miller and Miller, 2010) were lower than 5.5% (n = 5), indicating that the developed analytical method is highly accurate and precise.

To confirm the accuracy of the developed analytical method, i.e., trueness assessment, the analytical results were compared with that from our previously reported HPLC analysis (Hu et al., 2019). It was found that the results from the two methods showed good agreement, indicating that the developed analytical method is highly reliable and accurate for the determination of curcumin in real food samples.

The analytical method developed in this study enables an accurate determination of curcumin in dietary food samples by using BG-CQDs as a fluorescence nanoprobe. The interaction between BG-CQDs and curcumin was determined to be a combined effect of IFE and dynamic quenching. Under the optimal sensing conditions, good linearity was observed in the concentration ranges of 0.01–25.0 μM and the corresponding LOD obtained was low as 28.7 nM. The feasibility of the newly developed method for curcumin detection in the complex matrix was demonstrated in the analyses of real food samples with the spiked quantitative recoveries varying from 98.3% to 105.7%. Our proposed analytical method is remarkably simple, inexpensive and demonstrates operation ease, which would certainly increase the database for the ultra-sensitive detection of curcumin by using fluorescent CQDs as nanoprobe.

L-f.L., Q.H., and Z.Y. designed the experiments and wrote the manuscript. J.H. helped design the characterisation work. L-f.L., Q.H., and H.S. performed the experiments. L-f.L. Q.H. and Y.P. analysed data.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial supports from 2020 Postgraduate Research and Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province, China (SJCX20_1383), Natural Science Fund for Colleges and Universities in Jiangsu Province, China (19KJB150042), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21922202 and 31371806), Foundation of China National Key R&D Program (2016YFC1300201), Opening Foundation of Jiangsu Dairy Bioengineering Technology Research Centre (KYRY2019002), China KFC Catering Health Fund (2017) and Yangzhou University Science and Technology Innovation Team (2016) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jfca.2020.103639.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Afkhami et al., 2017

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A method based on ultrasound-assisted solidification of floating drop microextraction technique for the spectrophotometric determination of curcumin in turmeric powder

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Afkhami, A., Pirdadeh-Beiranvand, M., Madrakian, T. (2017). A method based on ultrasound-assisted solidification of floating drop microextraction technique for the spectrophotometric determination of curcumin in turmeric powder. Analytical and Bioanalytical Chemistry Research, 4(1), 1-10.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Ansari et al., 2005

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stability-indicating HPTLC determination of curcumin in bulk drug and pharmaceutical formulations

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ansari, M.J., Ahmad, S., Kohli, K., Ali, J., Khar, R.K. (2005). Stability-indicating HPTLC determination of curcumin in bulk drug and pharmaceutical formulations. Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis, 39(1), 132-138.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Baig and Chen, 2017

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bright carbon dots as fluorescence sensing agents for bacteria and curcumin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Baig, M.M.F., Chen, Y.-C. (2017). Bright carbon dots as fluorescence sensing agents for bacteria and curcumin. Journal of Colloid and Interface Science, 501, 341-349.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Baker and Baker, 2010
ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Baker, S.N., Baker, G.A. (2010). Luminescent carbon nanodots: emergent nanolights. Angewandte Chemie International Edition, 49(38), 6726-6744.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Bian et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as a sensitive fluorescent probe for the detection of curcumin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bian, W., Wang, X., Wang, Y., Yang, H., Huang, J., Cai, Z., Choi, M.M.F. (2018). Boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as a sensitive fluorescent probe for the detection of curcumin. Luminescence, 33(1), 174-180.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Chaisiwamongkhol et al., 2017

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multiwalled carbon nanotube modified electrodes for the adsorptive stripping voltammetric determination and quantification of curcumin in turmeric

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chaisiwamongkhol, K., Ngamchuea, K., Batchelor-McAuley, C., Compton, R.G. (2017). Multiwalled carbon nanotube modified electrodes for the adsorptive stripping voltammetric determination and quantification of curcumin in turmeric. Electroanalysis, 29(4), 1049-1055.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Chan et al., 2006

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dosage effects of curcumin on cell death types in a human osteoblast cell line

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chan, W.-H., Wu, H.-Y., Chang, W.H. (2006). Dosage effects of curcumin on cell death types in a human osteoblast cell line. Food and Chemical Toxicology, 44(8), 1362-1371.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Chen et al., 2012

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-sensitivity determination of curcumin in human urine using gemini zwitterionic surfactant as a probe by resonance light scattering technique

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen, X., Liang, S., Zhu, L., Liu, J., Zhang, G., Chen, Z. (2012). High-sensitivity determination of curcumin in human urine using gemini zwitterionic surfactant as a probe by resonance light scattering technique. Phytochemical Analysis, 23(5), 456-461.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Daneshgar et al., 2009

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of carbon nanotube and dysprosium nanowire modified electrodes as a sensor for determination of curcumin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Daneshgar, P., Norouzi, P., Moosavi-Movahedi, A.A., Ganjali, M.R., Haghshenas, E., Dousty, F., Farhadi, M. (2009). Fabrication of carbon nanotube and dysprosium nanowire modified electrodes as a sensor for determination of curcumin. Journal of Applied Electrochemistry, 39(10), 1983.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Gong et al., 2017

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An “on-off-on” fluorescent nanoprobe for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dot

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gong, X., Liu, Y., Yang, Z., Shuang, S., Zhang, Z., Dong, C. (2017). An “on-off-on” fluorescent nanoprobe for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dot. Analytica Chimica Acta, 968, 85-96.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Gong et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A di-functional and label-free carbon-based chem-nanosensor for real-time monitoring of pH fluctuation and quantitative determining of curcumin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gong, X., Wang, H., Liu, Y., Hu, Q., Gao, Y., Yang, Z., Shuang, S., Dong, C. (2019). A di-functional and label-free carbon-based chem-nanosensor for real-time monitoring of pH fluctuation and quantitative determining of curcumin. Analytica Chimica Acta, 1057, 132-144.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: He et al., 2018a

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of amine-modified magnetite-electrochemically reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode for sensitive dopamine determination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: He, Q., Liu, J., Liu, X., Li, G., Chen, D., Deng, P., Liang, J. (2018). Fabrication of amine-modified magnetite-electrochemically reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode for sensitive dopamine determination. Nanomaterials, 8, 194.

Manganese dioxide nanorods/electrochemically reduced graphene oxide nanocomposites modified electrodes for cost-effective and ultrasensitive detection of amaranth. Colloids and Surfaces B: Biointerfaces, 172, 565-572.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile and ultrasensitive determination of 4-nitrophenol based on acetylene black paste and graphene hybrid electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: He et al., 2019c

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile electrochemical sensor for nanomolar rutin detection based on magnetite nanoparticles and reduced graphene oxide decorated electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: He, Q., Wu, Y., Tian, Y., Li, G., Liu, J., Deng, P., Chen, D. (2019). Facile electrochemical sensor for nanomolar rutin detection based on magnetite nanoparticles and reduced graphene oxide decorated electrode. Nanomaterials, 9(1), 115.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hu, Q., Gao, L., Rao, S.-q., Yang, Z.-q., Li, T., Gong, X. (2019). Nitrogen and chlorine dual-doped carbon nanodots for determination of curcumin in food matrix via inner filter effect. Food Chemistry, 280, 195-202.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hu, Q., Liu, L.-f., Sun, H., Han, J., Gong, X., Liu, L., Yang, Z.-q. (2020a). An ultra-selective fluorescence method with enhanced sensitivity for the determination of manganese (VII) in food stuffs using carbon quantum dots as nanoprobe. Journal of Food Composition and Analysis, 88, 103447.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitivity enhanced fluorescence method for the detection of ferrocyanide ions in foodstuffs using carbon nanoparticles as sensing agents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hu, Q., Pan, Y., Gong, X., Rao, S.-q., Xiao, L., Liu, L., Yang, Z.-q. (2020b). A sensitivity enhanced fluorescence method for the detection of ferrocyanide ions in foodstuffs using carbon nanoparticles as sensing agents. Food Chemistry, 308, 125590.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Kharat et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancement of chemical stability of curcumin-enriched oil-in-water emulsions: Impact of antioxidant type and concentration

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kharat, M., Skrzynski, M., Decker, E.A., McClements, D.J. (2020). Enhancement of chemical stability of curcumin-enriched oil-in-water emulsions: Impact of antioxidant type and concentration. Food Chemistry, 320, 126653.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lee, J.H., Choung, M.-G. (2011). Determination of curcuminoid colouring principles in commercial foods by HPLC. Food Chemistry, 124(3), 1217-1222.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu, H., Ye, T., Mao, C. (2007). Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot. Angewandte Chemie International Edition, 46(34), 6473-6475.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu, L., Feng, F., Chin Paau, M., Hu, Q., Liu, Y., Chen, Z., Bai, Y., Guo, F., Choi, M.M.F. (2015). Sensitive determination of kaempferol using carbon dots as a fluorescence probe. Talanta, 144, 390-397.
